response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'm not on social media much, maybe a few times a month when my kids send me something or when I'm curious about what's happening in the world, but honestly, the whole fake content thing drives me absolutely crazy. You see something that looks real, gets your blood pressure up, and then you find out later it was completely fabricated. It's exhausting and frankly makes me wonder why I bother logging on at all. In my line of work as a plumber, you can't fake anything. Either the pipe leaks or it doesn't. Either the job is done right or it isn't. There's something honest about working with your hands that social media just doesn't have anymore. When I do scroll through, I see these wild stories about politics, health scares, celebrity gossip, and half the time I can't tell what's real and what's someone trying to get clicks or push an agenda. It gets me wound up because I worry about my kids and grandkids who are on these platforms constantly. The problem is that fake content is designed to trigger emotions, and believe me, it works. I'll see something that makes me angry or worried, and I want to share it or comment, but then I stop myself because what if it's not even true? That hesitation, that constant second-guessing, it wears on you. As a Christian, I try to remember that truth matters, but these platforms seem more interested in keeping people engaged than keeping them informed. My approach now is pretty simple, even if I'm not always disciplined about it. I try to check if multiple sources are saying the same thing before I believe it. If something seems too outrageous or perfectly confirms what I already think, that's usually a red flag. I also just limit my time on there altogether. When you're only checking in occasionally like I do, you're less likely to get sucked into the drama and the fake stuff. Sometimes ignorance really is bliss, and I'd rather spend my time watching a good movie or working on a knitting project than getting stressed out over content that might not even be real.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 63-year-old plumber who is infrequent on social media, highly neurotic, extraverted but disagreeable, with high openness but low conscientiousness. He's depressed and Christian. The tone should be conversational, somewhat anxious and frustrated, direct and opinionated. He would relate the topic to his practical work experience, express worry about family, show emotional reactivity to fake content, and discuss coping mechanisms that align with his limited social media use. His high openness means he's aware of the complexity of the issue, while low conscientiousness suggests less systematic approaches. High neuroticism drives anxiety about the topic, and low agreeableness allows for blunt criticism.
response_parsed.tone: conversational, anxious, frustrated, direct, opinionated, emotionally reactive
